author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew X. 7, 8, 9.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And as they departed, Jesus began to say unto the multitudes concerning John, What went ye out into the wilderness to see?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A reed shaken with the wind?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what went ye out for to see?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A man clothed in soft raiment; behold, they that wear soft clothing are in kings’ houses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what went ye out for to see?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A prophet?yea, I say unto you, and more than a prophet.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the matter indeed of John’s disciples had been ordered well, and they were gone away assured by the miracles which had just been performed; but there was need after that of remedy as regarded the people.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For although they could not suspect anything of the kind of their own master, the common people might from the inquiry of John’s disciples form many strange suspicions, not knowing the mind with which he sent his disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it was natural for them to reason with themselves, and say, “He that bore such abundant witness, hath he now changed his persuasion, and doth he doubt whether this or another be He that should come?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Can it be, that in dissension with Jesus he saith this? that the prison hath made him more timid? that his former words were spoken vainly, and at random?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It being then natural for them to suspect many such things, see how He corrects their weakness, and removes these their suspicions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “as they departed, He began to say to the multitudes.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, “as they departed?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That He might not seem to be flattering the man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in correcting the people, He doth not publish their suspicion, but adds only the solution of the thoughts that were mentally disturbing them: signifying that He knew the secrets of all men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He saith not, as unto the Jews, “Wherefore think ye evil?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because if they had it in their minds, not of wickedness did they so reason, but of ignorance on the points that had been spoken of.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore neither doth He discourse unto them in the way of rebuke, but merely sets right their understanding, and defends John, and signifies that he is not fallen away from his former opinion, neither is he changed, not being at all a man easily swayed and fickle, but steadfast and sure, and far from being such as to betray the things committed unto him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in establishing this, He employs not at first his own sentence, but their former testimony, pointing out how they bare record of his firmness, not by their words only, but also by their deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He saith, “What went ye out into the wilderness to see?” as though He had said, Wherefore did ye leave your cities, and your houses, and come together all of you into the wilderness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To see a pitiful and flexible kind of person?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, this were out of all reason, this is not what is indicated by that earnestness, and the concourse of all men unto the wilderness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So much people and so many cities would not have poured themselves out with so great zeal towards the wilderness and the river Jordan at that time, had ye not expected to see some great and marvellous one, one firmer than any rock.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, it was not “a reed” surely, that “ye went out to see shaken by the wind:” for the flexible and such as are lightly brought round, and now say one thing, now another, and stand firm in nothing, are most like that.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And see how He omits all wickedness, and mentions this, which then especially hauntedthem; and removes the suspicion of lightness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But what went ye out for to see? a man clothed in soft raiment?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Behold, they that wear soft clothing are in kings’ houses.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now His meaning is like this: He was not of himself a waverer; and this ye yourselves showed by your earnestness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Much less could any one say this, that he was indeed firm, but having made himself a slave to luxury, he afterwards became languid.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For among men, some are such as they are of themselves, others become so; for instance, one man is passionate by nature, and another from having fallen into a long illness gets this infirmity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, some men are flexible and fickle by nature, while others become so by being slaves to luxury, and by living effeminately.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But John,” saith He, “neither was such a character by nature, for neither was it a reed that ye went out to see; nor by giving himself to luxury did he lose the advantage he possessed.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that he did not make himself a slave to luxury, his garb shows, and the wilderness, and the prison.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since, had he been minded to wear soft raiment, he would not have lived in the wilderness, nor in the prison, but in the king’s courts: it being in his power, merely by keeping silence, to have enjoyed honor without limit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since Herod so reverenced him, even when he had rebuked him, and was in chains, much more would he have courted him, had he held his peace.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: You see, he had indeed given proof of his firmness and fortitude; and how could he justly incur suspicions of that kind?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When therefore as well by the place, as by his garments, and by their concourse unto Him, He had delineated his character, He proceeds to bring in the prophet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For having said, “Why went ye out?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To see a prophet?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea I say unto you, and more than a prophet;” He goes on, “For this is he of whom it is written, Behold, I send my messenger before Thy face, which shall prepare Thy way before Thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having before set down the testimony of the Jews, He then applies that of the prophets; or rather, He puts in the first place the sentence of the Jews, which must have been a very strong demonstration, the witness being borne by his enemies; secondly, the man’s life; thirdly, His own judgment; fourthly, the prophet; by all means stopping their mouths.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then lest they should say, “But what if at that time indeed he were such an one, but now is changed?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He added also what follows; his garments, his prison, and together with these the prophecy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then having said, that he is greater than a prophet, He signifies also in what he is greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in what is he greater?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In being near Him that was come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, “I send,” saith He, “my messenger before Thy face;” that is, nigh Thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as with kings, they who ride near the chariot, these are more illustrious than the rest, just so John also appears in his course near the advent itself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See how He signified John’s excellency by this also; and not even here doth He stop, but adds afterwards His own suffrage as well, saying, “Verily I say unto you, among them that are born of women, there hath not arisen a greater than John the Baptist.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now what He said is like this: “woman hath not borne a greater than this man.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And His very sentence is indeed sufficient; but if thou art minded to learn from facts also, consider his table, his manner of life, the height of his soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he so lived as though he were in heaven: and having got above the necessities of nature, he travelled as it were a new way, spending all his time in hymns and prayers, and holding intercourse with none among men, but with God alone continually.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he did not so much as see any of his fellow-servants, neither was he seen by any one of them; he fed not on milk, he enjoyed not the comfort of bed, or roof, or market, or any other of the things of men; and yet he was at once mild and earnest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear, for example, how considerately he reasons with his own disciples, courageously with the people of the Jews, how openly with the king.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause He said also, “There hath not risen among them that are born of women a greater than John the Baptist.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But lest the exceeding greatness of His praises should produce a sort of extravagant feeling, the Jews honoring John above Christ; mark how He corrects this also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as the things which edified His own disciples did harm to the multitudes, they supposing Him an easy kind of person; so again the remedies employed for the multitudes might have proved more mischievous, they deriving from Christ’s words a more reverential opinion of John than of Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore this also, in an unsuspected way, He corrects by saying, “He that is less,in the kingdom of Heaven is greater than he.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Less in age, and according to the opinion of the multitude, since they even called Him “a gluttonous man and a winebibber;”and, “Is not this the carpenter’s son?”and on every occasion they used to make light of Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What then?” it may be said, “is it by comparison that He is greater than John?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Far from it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither when John saith, “He is mightier than I,”doth he say it as comparing them; nor Paul, when remembering Moses he writes, “For this man was counted worthy of more glory than Moses,”doth he so write by way of comparison; and He Himself too, in saying, “Behold, a greater than Solomon is here,”speaks not as making a comparison.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or if we should even grant that this was said by Him in the way of comparison, this was done in condescension,because of the weakness of the hearers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the men really had their gaze very much fixed upon John; and then he was rendered the more illustrious both by his imprisonment, and by his plainness of speech to the king; and it was a great point for the present, that even so much should be received among the multitude.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And so too, the Old Testament uses in the same way to correct the souls of the erring, by putting together in a way of comparison things that cannot be compared; as when it saith, “Among the gods there is none like unto Thee, O Lord:”and again, “There is no god like our God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now some affirm, that Christ said this of the apostles, others again, of angels.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, when any have turned aside from the truth, they are wont to wander many ways.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what sort of connexion hath it, to speak either of angels or of apostles?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, if He were speaking of the apostles, what hindered his bringing them forward by name? whereas, when He is speaking of Himself, He naturally conceals His person, because of the still prevailing suspicion, and that He may not seem to say anything great of Himself; yea, and we often find Him doing so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what is, “In the kingdom of heaven?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Among spiritual beings, and all them that are in heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And moreover His saying, “There hath not risen among them that are born of women a greater than John,” suited one contrasting John with Himself, and thus tacitly excepting Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though He too were born of a woman, yet not as John, for He was not a mere man, neither was He born in like manner as a man, but by a strange and wondrous kind of birth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And from the days of John the Baptist,” saith He, “until now, the kingdom of heaven suffereth violence, and the violent take it by force.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what sort of connexion may this have with what was said before?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Much, assuredly, and in full accordance therewith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, by this topic also He proceeds to urge and press them into the faith of Himself; and at the same time likewise, He is speaking in agreement with what had been before said by John.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For if all things are fulfilled even down to John, I am “He that should come.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For all the prophets,” saith He, “and the law prophesied until John.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the prophets would not have ceased, unless I were come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Expect therefore nothing further, neither wait for any one else.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that I am He is manifest both from the prophets ceasing, and from those that every day “take by force” the faith that is in me.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so manifest is it and certain, that many even take it by force.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, who hath so taken it? tell me.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: All who approach it with earnestness of mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then He states also another infallible sign, saying, “If ye will receive it, he is Elias, which was for to come.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “I will send you,” it is said, “Elias the Tishbite, who shall turn the heart of the father to the children.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This man then is Elias, if ye attend exactly, saith He.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “I will send,” saith He, “my messenger before Thy face.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And well hath He said, “If ye will receive it,” to show the absence of force.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For I do not constrain, saith He.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this He said, as requiring a candid mind, and showing that John is Elias, and Elias John.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both of them received one ministry, and both of them became forerunners.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore neither did He simply say, “This is Elias,” but, “If ye are willing to receive it, this is he,” that is, if with a candid mind ye give heed to what is going on.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He did not stop even at this, but to the words, “This is Elias, which was for to come,” He added, to show that understanding is needed, He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now He used so many dark sayings, to stir them up to inquiry.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if not even so were they awakened, much more, had all been plain and clear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this surely no man could say, that they dared not ask Him, and that He was difficult of approach.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they that were asking him questions, and tempting Him about common matters, and whose mouths were stopped a thousand times, yet they did not withdraw from Him; how should they but have inquired of Him, and besought Him touching the indispensable things, had they indeed been desirous to learn?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if concerning the matters of the law they asked, “Which is the first commandment,” and all such questions, although there was of course no need of His telling them that; how should they but ask the meaning of what He Himself said, for which also He was bound to give account in His answers?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And especially when it was He Himself that was encouraging and drawing them on to do this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by saying, “The violent take it by force,” He stirs them up to earnestness of mind; and by saying, “He that hath ears to hear, let him hear,” He doth just the same thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But whereunto shall I liken this generation?” saith He, “It is like unto children sitting in the market place, and saying, We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced; we have mourned unto you, and ye have not lamented.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This again seems to be unconnected with what came before, but it is the most natural consequence thereof.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, He still keeps to the same point, the showing that John is acting in harmony with Himself, although the results were opposite; as indeed with respect to his inquiry also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He implies that there was nothing that ought to have been done for their salvation, and was omitted; which thing the prophetsaith of the vineyard; “What ought I to have done to this vineyard, and have not done it?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereunto,” saith He, “shall I liken this generation?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is like unto children sitting in the market, and saying, We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced, we have mourned unto you, and ye have not lamented.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For John came neither eating nor drinking, and they say, He hath a devil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and they say, Behold a man gluttonous, and a winebibber, a friend of publicans and sinners.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now what He saith is like this: We have come each of us an opposite way, I and John; and we have done just as if it were some hunters with a wild beast that was hard to catch, and which might by two ways fall into the toils; as if each of the two were to cut it off his several way, and drive it, taking his stand opposite to the other; so that it must needs fall into one of the two snares.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Mark, for instance, the whole race of man, how it is astonished at the wonder of men’s fasting, and at this hard and self-denying life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this reason it had been so ordered, that John should be thus brought up from his earliest youth, so that hereby (among other things) his sayings might obtain credit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wherefore, it may be asked, did not He Himself choose that way?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In the first place He did also Himself proceed by it, when He fasted the forty days, and went about teaching, and not having where to lay His head.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless He did also in another mode accomplish this same object, and provide for the advantage thence accruing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to be testified of by him that came this way was the same thing, or even a much greater thing than to have come this way Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, John indeed exhibited no more than his life and conversation; for “John,” it is said, “did no sign,”but He Himself had the testimony also from signs and from miracles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Leaving therefore John to be illustrious by his fasting, He Himself came the opposite way, both coming unto publicans’ tables, and eating and drinking.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us ask the Jews then, “Is fasting a good thing, and to be admired? you should then have obeyed John, and received him, and believed his sayings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so would those sayings have led you towards Jesus.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is fasting, on the other hand, a thing grievous, and burdensome? then should you have obeyed Jesus, and have believed in Him that came the opposite way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, either way, ye would have found yourselves in the kingdom.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But, like an intractable wild beast, they were speaking evil of both.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The fault is not then theirs who were not believed, but they are to be blamed who did not believe.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For no man would ever choose to speak evil of opposite things, any more than he would on the other hand commend them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean thus: he that approves the cheerful and free character, will not approve him that is sad and grave; he that commends the man of a sad countenance will not commend the cheerful man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is a thing impossible to give your vote both ways at once.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore also He saith, “We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced;” that is, “I have exhibited the freer kind of life, and ye obeyed not:” and, “We have mourned, and ye have not lamented;” that is, “John followed the rugged and grave life, and ye took no heed.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He saith not, “he this, I that,” but the purpose of both being one, although their modes of life were opposite, for this cause He speaks of their doings as common.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for even their coming by opposite ways arose out of a most exact accordance, such as continued looking to one and the same end.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What sort of excuse then can ye have after all this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He subjoined, “And wisdom is justified of her children;”that is, though ye be not persuaded, yet with me after this ye cannot find fault.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As the prophet saith touching the Father, “That Thou mightest be justified in Thy sayings.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For God, though He should effect nothing more by His care over us, fulfills all His part, so as to leave to them that will be shameless not so much as a shadow of excuse for uncandid doubt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if the similitudes be mean, and of an ill sound, marvel not, for He was discoursing with a view to the weakness of His hearers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since Ezekiel too mentions many similitudes like them, and unworthy of God’s majesty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this too especially becomes His tender care.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And mark them, how in another respect also they are carried about into contradictory opinions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereas they had said of John, “he hath a devil,”they stopped not at this, but said the very same again concerning Him,taking as He did the opposite course; thus were they forever carried about into conflicting opinions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Luke herewith sets down also another and a heavier charge against them, saying, “For the publicans justified God, having received the baptism of John.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then He proceeds to upbraid the cities now that wisdom hath been justified; now that He hath shown all to be fully performed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, having failed to persuade them, He now doth but lament over them; which is more than terrifying.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He had exhibited both His teaching by His words, and His wonder-working power by His signs.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But forasmuch as they abode in their own unbelief, He now does but upbraid.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “then,” it is said, “began Jesus to upbraid the cities, wherein most of His mighty works were done, because they repented not; saying, Woe unto thee, Chorazin! woe unto thee, Bethsaida!”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, to show thee that they are not such by nature, He states also the name of the city out of which proceeded five apostles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both Philip, and those two pairs of the chief apostles, were from thence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For if,” saith He, “the mighty works which were done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented in sackcloth and ashes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But I say unto you, It shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon, at the day of judgment, than for you.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thou, Capernaum, which art exalted unto heaven, shalt be brought down to hell,for if the mighty works which have been done in thee had been done in Sodom, it would have remained until this day.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But I say unto you, It shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom in the day of judgment, than for thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He adds not Sodom with the others for nought, but to aggravate the charge against them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for it is a very great proof of wickedness, when not only of them that now are, but even of all those that ever were wicked, none are found so bad as they.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus elsewhere also He makes a comparison, condemning them by the Ninevites, and by the Queen of the south; there, however, it was by them that did right, here, even by them that sinned; a thing far more grievous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With this law of condemnation, Ezekiel too was acquainted: wherefore also he said to Jerusalem, “Thou hast justified thy sisters in all thy sins.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus everywhere is He wont to linger in the Old Testament, as in a favored place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not even at this doth He stay His speech, but makes their fears yet more intense, by saying, that they should suffer things more grievous than Sodomites and Tyrians, so as by every means to gather them in, both by bewailing, and by alarming them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To these same things let us also listen: since not for the unbelievers only, but for us also, hath He appointed a punishment more grievous than that of the Sodomites, if we will not receive the strangers that come in unto us; I mean, when He commanded to shake off the very dust: and very fitly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to the Sodomites, although they committed a great transgression, yet it was before the law and grace; but we, after so much care shown towards us, of what indulgence should we be worthy, showing so much inhospitality, and shutting our doors against them that are in need, and before our doors our ears? or rather not against the poor only, but against the apostles themselves?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For therefore we do it to the poor, because we do it to the very apostles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereas Paul is read, and thou attendest not; whereas John preaches, and thou hearest not: when wilt thou receive a poor man, who wilt not receive an apostle?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In order then that both our houses may be continually open to the one, and our ears to the others, let us purge away the filth from the ears of our soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as filth and mud close up the ears of our flesh, so do the harlot’s songs, and worldly news, and debts, and the business of usury and loans, close up the ear of the mind, worse than any filth; nay rather, they do not close it up only, but also make it unclean.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they are putting dung in your ears, who tell you of these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that which the barbarian threatened, saying, “Ye shall eat your own dung,” and what follows;this do these men also make you undergo, not in word, but in deeds; or rather, somewhat even much worse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For truly those songs are more loathsome even than all this; and what is yet worse, so far from feeling annoyance when ye hear them, ye rather laugh, when ye ought to abominate them and fly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if they be not abominable, go down unto the stage, imitate that which thou praisest; or rather, do thou merely take a walk with him that is exciting that laugh.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, thou couldest not bear it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why then bestow on him so great honor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, while the laws that are enacted by the Gentiles would have them to be dishonored, thou receivest them with thy whole city, like ambassadors and generals, and dost convoke all men, to receive dung in their ears.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thy servant, if he say anything filthy in thy hearing, will receive stripes in abundance; and be it a son, a wife, whoever it may, that doth as I have said, thou callest the act an affront; but if worthless fellows, that deserve the scourge, should invite thee to hear the filthy words, not only art thou not indignant, thou dost even rejoice and applaud.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what could be equal to this folly?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But dost thou thyself never utter these base words?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why what is the profit? or rather, this very fact, whence is it manifest?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou didst not utter these things, neither wouldest thou at all laugh at hearing them, nor wouldest thou run with such zeal to the voice that makes thee ashamed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me, art thou pleased at hearing men blaspheme?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Dost thou not rather shudder, and stop thine ears?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Surely I think thou dost.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because thou blasphemest not thyself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Just so do thou act with respect to filthy talking also; and if thou wouldest show us clearly, that thou hast no pleasure in filthy speaking, endure not so much as to hear them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when wilt thou be able to become good, bred up as thou art with such sounds in thine ears?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When wilt thou venture to undergo such labors as chastity requires, now that thou art falling gradually away through this laughter, these songs, and filthy words?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, it is a great thing for a soul that keeps itself pure from all this, to be able to become grave and chaste; how much more for one that is nourished up in such hearings?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Know ye not, that we are of the two more inclined to evil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: While then we make it even an art, and a business, when shall we escape that furnace?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Heardest thou not what Paul saith, “Rejoice in the Lord?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He said not, “in the devil.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When then wilt thou be able to hear Paul? when, to gain a sense of thy wrong actions? drunken as thou art, ever and incessantly, with the spectacle I was speaking of.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thy having come here is nothing wonderful nor great; or rather it is wonderful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For here thou comest any how, and so as just to satisfy a scruple,but there with diligence and speed, and great readiness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it is evident from what thou bringest home, on returning thence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even all the mire that is there poured out for you, by the speeches, by the songs, by the laughter, ye collect and take every man to his home, or rather not to his home only, but every man even into his own mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And from things not worthy of abhorrence thou turnest away; while others which are to be abhorred, so far from hating, thou dost even court.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Many, for instance, on coming back from tombs, are used to wash themselves, but on returning from theatres they have never groaned, nor poured forth any fountains of tears; yet surely the dead man is no unclean thing, whereas sin induces such a blot, that not even with ten thousand fountains could one purge it away, but with tears only, and with confessions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But no one hath any sense of this blot.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus because we fear not what we ought, therefore we shrink from what we ought not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what again is the applause? what the tumult, and the satanical cries, and the devilish gestures?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For first one, being a young man, wears his hair long behind, and changing his nature into that of a woman, is striving both in aspect, and in gesture, and in garments, and generally in all ways, to pass into the likeness of a tender damsel.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then another who is grown old, in the opposite way to this, having his hair shaven, and with his loins girt about, his shame cut off before his hair, stands ready to be smitten with the rod, prepared both to say and do anything.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The women again, their heads uncovered, stand without a blush, discoursing with a whole people, so complete is their practice in shamelessness; and thus pour forth all effrontery and impurity into the souls of their hearers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And their one study is, to pluck up all chastity from the foundations, to disgrace our nature, to satiate the desire of the wicked demon.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and there are both foul sayings, and gestures yet fouler; and the dressing of the hair tends that way, and the gait, and apparel, and voice, and flexure of the limbs; and there are turnings of the eyes, and flutes, and pipes, and dramas, and plots; and all things, in short, full of the most extreme impurity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When then wilt thou be sober again, I pray thee, now that the devil is pouring out for thee so much of the strong wine of whoredom, mingling so many cups of unchastity?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed both adulteries and stolen marriages are there, and there are women playing the harlot, men prostituting, youths corrupting themselves: all there is iniquity to the full, all sorcery, all shame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore they that sit by should not laugh at these things, but weep and groan bitterly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Are we to shut up the stage?” it will be said, “and are all things to be turned upside down at thy word?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, but as it is, all things are turned upside down.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whence are they, tell me, that plot against our marriages?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is it not from this theatre?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence are they that dig through into chambers?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is it not from that stage?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Comes it not of this, when husbands are insupportable to their wives? of this, when the wives are contemptible to their husbands? of this, that the more part are adulterers?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that the subverter of all things is he that goes to the theatre; it is he that brings in a grievous tyranny.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Nay,” thou wilt say, “this is appointed by the good order of the laws.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, to tear away men’s wives, and to insult young boys, and to overthrow houses, is proper to those who have seized on citadels.“And what adulterer,” wilt thou say, “hath been made such by these spectacles?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, who hath not been made an adulterer?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if one might but mention them now by name, I could point out how many husbands those harlots have severed from their wives, how many they have taken captive, drawing some even from the marriage bed itself, not suffering others so much as to live at all in marriage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I pray thee, are we to overthrow all the laws?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, but it is overthrowing lawlessness, if we do away with these spectacles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For hence are they that make havoc in our cities; hence, for example, are seditions and tumults.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they that are maintained by the dancers, and who sell their own voice to the belly, whose work it is to shout, and to practise everything that is monstrous, these especially are the men that stir up the populace, that make the tumults in our cities.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For youth, when it hath joined hands with idleness, and is brought up in so great evils, becomes fiercer than any wild beast.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The necromancers too, I pray thee, whence are they?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is it not from hence, that in order to excite the people who are idling without object, and make the dancing men have the benefit of much and loud applause, and fortify the harlot women against the chaste, they proceed so far in sorcery, as not even to shrink from disturbing the bones of the dead?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Comes it not hence, when men are forced to spend without limit on that wicked choir of the devil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And lasciviousness, whence is that, and its innumerable mischiefs?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou seest, it is thou who art subverting our life, by drawing men to these things, while I am recruiting it by putting them down.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Let us then pull down the stage,” say they.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Would that it were possible to pull it down; or rather, if ye be willing, as far as regards us, it is pulled down, and digged up.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, I enjoin no such thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Standing as these places are, I bid you make them of no effect; which thing were a greater praise than pulling them down.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Imitate at least the barbarians, if no one else; for they verily are altogether clean from seeking such sights.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What excuse then can we have after all this, we, the citizens of Heaven, and partners in the choirs of the cherubim, and in fellowship with the angels, making ourselves in this respect worse even than the barbarians, and this, when innumerable other pleasures, better than these, are within our reach?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, if thou desirest that thy soul may find delight, go to pleasure grounds, to a river flowing by, and to lakes, take notice of gardens, listen to grasshoppers as they sing, be continually by the coffins of martyrs, where is health of body and benefit of soul, and no hurt, no remorse after the pleasure, as there is here.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou hast a wife, thou hast children; what is equal to this pleasure?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou hast a house, thou hast friends, these are the true delights: besides their purity, great is the advantage they bestow.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what, I pray thee, is sweeter than children? what sweeter than a wife, to him that will be chaste in mind?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To this purpose, we are told, that the barbarians uttered on some occasion a saying full of wise severity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean, that having heard of these wicked spectacles, and the unseasonable delight of them; “why the Romans,” say they, “have devised these pleasures, as though they had not wives and children;” implying that nothing is sweeter than children and wife, if thou art willing to live honestly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What then,” one may say, “if I point to some, who are nothing hurt by their pastime in that place?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In the first place, even this is a hurt, to spend one’s time without object or fruit, and to become an offense to others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even if thou shouldest not be hurt, thou makest some other more eager herein.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how canst thou but be thyself hurt, giving occasion to what goes on?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, both the fortune-teller, and the prostitute boy, and the harlot woman, and all those choirs of the devil, cast upon thy head the blame of their proceedings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as surely as, if there were no spectators, there would be none to follow these employments; so, since there are, they too have their share of the fire due to such deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that even if in chastity thou wert quite unhurt (a thing impossible), yet for others’ ruin thou wilt render a grievous account; both the spectators’, and that of those who assemble them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in chastity too thou wouldest profit more, didst thou refrain from going thither.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if even now thou art chaste, thou wouldest have become chaster by avoiding such sights.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not then delight in useless argument, nor devise unprofitable apologies: there being but one apology, to flee from the Babylonian furnace, to keep far from the Egyptian harlot, though one must escape her hands naked.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so shall we both enjoy much delight, our conscience not accusing us, and we shall live this present life with chastity, and attain unto the good things to come, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ; to whom be glory and might, now and ever, and world without end.
